title: Built an automated futures trading system over the last ~15 months — curious what experienced traders think
author: Iulian-SavantTrading
selftext:
Hey everyone,

Over the last \~15 months we’ve been building and refining an automated futures trading system for NinjaTrader based on our own manual trading experience.

The system combines fixed-rule execution with concepts related to market structure, liquidity behavior and SMC-style logic.

The main objective from the beginning was not to create a “holy grail”, but rather something capable of surviving different market conditions while removing the emotional side of execution entirely.

Instead of discretionary trading, the focus was placed on:

• predefined execution rules  
• controlled risk parameters  
• session-based filtering  
• automated management  
• consistency over long periods

The system currently runs multiple models across futures markets including indices, forex, crypto, metals and bonds.

We’ve mainly been testing robustness, behavior during drawdowns and long-term stability rather than trying to aggressively optimize for unrealistic returns.

Internal hypothetical/backtested data currently covers:

**01 Jan 2025 → 22 Apr 2026**

Some examples from different models:

• NQ models reaching around \~$40K simulated net profit with high win-rate behavior  
• ES models showing lower volatility and more controlled equity curves  
• Treasury/Bond models performing surprisingly stable during slower market conditions  
• Separate crypto and forex models behaving very differently depending on volatility regimes

Obviously these are hypothetical/backtested results, not audited live returns, and we fully understand the limitations that come with backtesting.

One thing we noticed during development is that systems with lower excitement but stronger consistency tend to survive much longer than highly optimized systems that look amazing for short periods.

Curious what more experienced traders here think about:

• balancing robustness vs optimization  
• acceptable drawdown levels for futures automation  
• differences between prop-firm behavior vs live-account behavior  
• whether simpler fixed-rule systems tend to outperform more “intelligent” adaptive systems long-term

Happy to discuss the development process and hear different opinions from people running futures automation themselves.

comments.author: eggrally
comments.body: have you tested it with real money,  back testing means nothing

replies.author: Iulian-SavantTrading
replies.body: yes, last 6-7 months i did forward testing in market conditions, on my accounts, so we can say 2025 is backtest and 2026 is live behaviour

comments.author: Spare_Subject_7069
comments.body: where did you source your historical data? databento?

comments.author: Temporary_Fun_7973
comments.body:
Honestly I'm not sure how will it go with audusd, how do u deal with spread? I'm building trading bot myself and I choose eurusd bc of spread on other pairs that can be very deadly on less population options. 

If u did good on live test (demo acc) and had around 200+ trades on it u are good to start smaller amount of real money. Just take it slowly with investment, don't rush big numbers they will come eventually. 

U can also make a smaller real money acc (50/100$) and set your bot to risk 1% of the acc total balance. It will boost your profit alot and u will get much faster to some good amount of money.

replies.author: Iulian-SavantTrading
replies.body: you are wright, forex "eat" a lot of your proffit, but for me forex is just like an passive income, not the main EA. My "Heavy traders" are indices Nq and ES

replies.author: Temporary_Fun_7973
replies.body: I get u, I'm just suggesting to use maybe EURUSD for bot and for manual trading use AUDUSD, JPYUSD, ect. But if u find a way to deal with spread and all limitations on other pairs u are good than.

replies.author: Iulian-SavantTrading
replies.body: i also have a bot template for eurusd

comments.author: celzo1776
comments.body: how did you avoid config drift on a 2 year running Vibe coded project?

replies.author: Iulian-SavantTrading
replies.body:
Honestly, we avoided config drift mostly by being very strict with versioning and by not letting the live environment become the “source of truth.”

Every major change was checkpointed, configs were kept tied to specific strategy/template versions, and we avoided random production tweaks without documenting them first.

The project evolved a lot, but the rule was simple: strategy logic, risk parameters and deployment settings had to stay reproducible. If something changed, it had to be traceable.

Also, we separated core execution logic from runtime parameters as much as possible, so different models could be adjusted without silently changing the behavior of the whole system.

Not perfect, but discipline around backups, checkpoints, versioned builds and controlled releases saved us from most of the chaos that usually happens in long-running “vibe coded” projects.

replies.author: Iulian-SavantTrading
replies.body: we don't provide demo. on website there are statistics and all you need tounderstand if it is what you need. There is one pach with a monthly fee, to "try" and the rest is LIFETIME fee.

comments.author: sofa_king_ape
comments.body: just curious what type of model are you using and why? did you try or consider any others?

replies.author: Iulian-SavantTrading
replies.body:
Thank you, really appreciate it.

Our models are mostly rule-based systematic intraday futures models derived from manual trading behavior rather than purely statistical black-box systems.

A large part of the logic revolves around:  
• market structure behavior  
• liquidity reactions  
• volatility conditions  
• session timing  
• imbalance/FVG-type behavior  
• momentum continuation or failure  
• risk asymmetry

The reason we chose this direction is because it was the most “understandable” and reproducible approach coming from discretionary futures trading.

We did explore and test other ideas as well:  
• more adaptive/filter-heavy systems  
• higher-frequency concepts  
• statistical approaches  
• more AI/ML-oriented filtering  
• complex multi-confirmation models

But interestingly, many of the more sophisticated systems became harder to generalize and more fragile out-of-sample.

One of the biggest lessons for us was that simpler models with strong risk control and repeatable execution often survive longer than highly “intelligent” systems optimized for ideal conditions.

So over time, the project evolved more toward robustness and survivability rather than maximum theoretical performance.
